title: Understanding Variance of Estimator
text: We are now finding the variance of the estimator a-hat, not its bias. Variance tells us how much the estimator changes from sample to sample. A smaller variance means the estimator is more stable.

title: Variance Rule for Constants
text:
If c is a constant, then:
$$Var(cX)=c**{2}Var(X)$$
Using this:
$$Var(\hat{\alpha})=Var\left( rac{1}{100}\sum_{i=1}**{100}X_iight)= rac{1}{100**{2}}Var\left(\sum_{i=1}**{100}X_iight)$$

title: Independence in Context of Variance
text: Because the $$X_i$$ are independent, their covariance is $$0$$. So, the variance of their sum is simply the sum of their variances. Using this:\n$$\frac{1}{100**{2}}Var\left(\sum_{i=1}**{100}X_i\right)=\frac{1}{100**{2}}(Var(X_1)+Var(X_2)+\ldots+Var(X_{100}))$$

title: Use the Common Variance Value
text: The problem says each observation has the same variance of σ². So, the sum of variances becomes $$100$$ copies of σ²:\n$$Var(X_1)+Var(X_2)+\ldots+Var(X_{100})=100\sigma**{2}$$

title: Understanding Variance Conceptually
text: This shows that averaging $$100$$ independent observations reduces variability by a factor of $$100$$. $$Var(a-hat)$$ describes how much the sample mean would vary across repeated random samples of $$100$$ students. Even if individual students vary a lot, averaging many students makes the estimator more stable.
